past_medical_history: Lumbar disc herniation (L4-L5), diagnosed 6 years ago, managed conservatively with physical therapy
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago, on oral hypoglycemics, HbA1c 7.8% three months ago
Hypertension, diagnosed 12 years ago, well controlled on medication
Chronic kidney disease stage 2, baseline creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Remote splenectomy at age 52 for idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, did not receive recommended post-splenectomy vaccinations
Mild cognitive impairment, diagnosed 18 months ago, under outpatient neurology follow-up

medications.frequency: three times daily (started 3 days ago by local clinic)

family_history: Mother had Alzheimer's disease, diagnosed at age 78, died at 85
Father had type 2 diabetes, died of stroke at age 72
No family history of immunodeficiency disorders

social_history.alcohol: Occasional soju, less than once per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her husband in a second-floor apartment; daughter lives in the same city and visits weekly
social_history.exercise: Daily walks in the neighborhood park, approximately 20 minutes, has decreased over the past month due to unsteadiness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Drowsy but arousable to voice. Oriented to person only; confused about place and date. Unable to recall three words at five minutes. Speech is slow but coherent when she responds. Follows simple one-step commands inconsistently. Cannot perform serial subtractions. K-MMSE estimated at 15 (baseline 22 eighteen months ago). GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive to light but sluggish. Visual fields grossly intact to confrontation when patient cooperates. No gaze deviation. Mild bilateral facial grimace to stimulation, symmetric. No obvious facial droop. Fundoscopic exam limited by cooperation; no papilledema appreciated. Gag reflex intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.motor: Moves all four extremities spontaneously but less vigorously than expected. Upper extremities: 4+/5 strength bilaterally in proximal and distal muscle groups, limited by cooperation. Lower extremities: 4/5 bilaterally, again limited by effort. No pronator drift assessable. No fasciculations or atrophy noted. Tone is mildly increased diffusely.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws to pain symmetrically in all extremities. Light touch sensation appears grossly intact but examination is unreliable due to altered sensorium. Proprioception not reliably tested.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 3+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are extensor (upgoing) on the left and equivocal on the right.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose mildly dysmetric bilaterally, though limited by decreased alertness. Heel-to-shin not reliably performed.
neurological_exam.gait: Previously noted to have a shuffling, small-step gait with difficulty turning on prior clinic visits. Currently unable to stand unassisted due to somnolence and unsteadiness. Required wheelchair transport to the emergency department.
neurological_exam.additional: Nuchal rigidity present — moderate resistance to passive neck flexion. Kernig sign positive bilaterally. Brudzinski sign positive — involuntary hip and knee flexion with passive neck flexion. No petechiae or purpura on skin examination. No lymphadenopathy. Small well-healed left upper quadrant surgical scar consistent with prior splenectomy.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive confusion, unsteady gait, and low-grade fever over the past several days
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Soon-Ja Park is a 74-year-old right-handed Korean woman with a history of remote splenectomy for idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage 2, lumbar disc herniation, and previously diagnosed mild cognitive impairment who presents to the emergency department with progressive confusion and worsening gait instability over the past ten days, with new-onset low-grade fever over the past three days. According to her husband, the patient first began to show noticeable decline in her gait approximately five weeks ago, developing a shuffling, small-step pattern with frequent stumbles and near-falls. She was seen at an outpatient neurology clinic three weeks ago, where her gait abnormalities and worsening memory difficulties raised concern for possible normal pressure hydrocephalus given her age and clinical presentation. Brain imaging at that visit was noted to show ventricular enlargement, and further workup was planned. However, over the past ten days, her cognitive function has deteriorated more rapidly than expected. She has become increasingly confused, forgetting the names of family members, repeating questions, and on two occasions becoming disoriented in her own apartment. Five days ago, she developed a mild cough and nasal congestion. Her husband took her to a local primary care clinic three days ago, where she was diagnosed with an upper respiratory infection and started on oral amoxicillin-clavulanate. Despite the antibiotic treatment, she developed a low-grade fever two days ago, measured at 38.1 degrees Celsius at home. She began complaining of a diffuse headache that was not relieved by acetaminophen. Yesterday evening, she became notably more lethargic, had difficulty staying awake during dinner, and vomited once. Her husband also observed that she seemed to flinch and turn away from bright lights. This morning, she was difficult to rouse from sleep and could only state her name when questioned. Her husband called emergency services. In the emergency department, she intermittently opens her eyes to voice and follows simple commands inconsistently. She complains of neck pain when asked. The emergency physician notes her prior history of gait disturbance and cognitive decline and considers whether this represents progression of her suspected normal pressure hydrocephalus, but the acuity of the recent deterioration and new fever raise concern for an infectious process. Her asplenic status and diabetic history are noted as risk factors for encapsulated organism infections.

findings.location: Bilateral lateral ventricles, third ventricle, and fourth ventricle

signal_characteristics.T2: CSF signal within dilated ventricles without periventricular signal abnormality
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No periventricular transependymal edema or flow voids. Mild periventricular white matter hyperintensities consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic changes

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of the cortical sulci at the high convexity with relatively crowded vertex sulci, disproportionate to calvarial volume

findings.location: Subtle, patchy leptomeningeal enhancement noted along bilateral frontoparietal convexities and within the basal cisterns

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Thin, discontinuous linear enhancement along the pial surfaces, more prominent in the basal cisterns and Sylvian fissures than over the convexities
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle sulcal FLAIR hyperintensity in bilateral frontoparietal regions, which may represent proteinaceous or cellular material in the subarachnoid space
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion within the brain parenchyma. No evidence of cerebritis, abscess, or acute infarction

findings.location: Periventricular and subcortical white matter, bilateral

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense foci in periventricular white matter
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Confluent periventricular hyperintensities with scattered subcortical punctate lesions

additional_observations: No subdural empyema or epidural collection identified
Mild generalized cerebral volume loss appropriate for age
Normal flow voids in the major intracranial arteries
mri.impression: However, subtle patchy leptomeningeal enhancement in the basal cisterns and along the frontoparietal convexities raises concern for an infectious or inflammatory meningeal process. No evidence of cerebritis, abscess formation, or acute infarction. Chronic small vessel ischemic changes (Fazekas grade 2). Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 18.7 x10^3/uL (H), Neutrophils 84.2 % (H), Bands 8 % (H), Lymphocytes 5.3 % (L), Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 33.8 % (L), Sodium 131 mEq/L (L), Chloride 97 mEq/L (L), Bicarbonate 20 mEq/L (L), BUN 28 mg/dL (H), Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (H), Glucose 227 mg/dL (H), CRP 142 mg/L (H), Procalcitonin 3.8 ng/mL (H), ESR 78 mm/hr (H), PT 13.8 seconds (H), INR 1.2  (H), D-dimer 2.8 ug/mL (H), Albumin 3.1 g/dL (L), TSH 5.8 mIU/L (H), Lactate 2.6 mmol/L (H).

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 28 cm. WBC: 380 cells/mm3 (68% PMN/28% lymph). Protein: 178 mg/dL. Glucose: 32 mg/dL (ratio 0.14 (serum glucose 227 mg/dL)). Gram_stain: No organisms identified (negative). India_ink: Negative. Cryptococcal_antigen: Negative. AFB_stain: Negative. TB_PCR: Negative. Latex_agglutination: Positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae antigen.

special_tests.Latex_agglutination: Weakly positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae antigen

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 18 cm. WBC: 85 cells/mm3 (decreased from 380) (35% PMN/58% lymph). Protein: 98 mg/dL. Glucose: 52 mg/dL (ratio 0.35 (serum glucose 148 mg/dL)). Gram_stain: No organisms identified. Bacterial_culture: No growth at 48 hours. Latex_agglutination: Weakly positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae antigen.

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of cortical sulci at vertex

output.impression: Non-contrast CT head demonstrates communicating hydrocephalus without evidence of acute hemorrhage, mass lesion, or herniation. Safe to proceed with lumbar puncture. Chronic small vessel ischemic changes noted. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Aerobic bottle Streptococcus pneumoniae (penicillin MIC 0.06 ug/mL — susceptible)  (H), Time to positivity 14 hours hours (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Blood culture positive: Streptococcus pneumoniae (aerobic bottle, 14 hours) (H)

output.query: bacterial meningitis treatment guidelines pneumococcal asplenic

results.title: Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis
results.authors: Tunkel AR, Hartman BJ, Kaplan SL, et al.
results.key_finding: Empiric therapy for community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults >50 years: vancomycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime) plus ampicillin (for Listeria coverage). Adjunctive dexamethasone should be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics.

results.title: Pneumococcal meningitis in adults after introduction of PCV13
results.authors: Castelblanco RL, Lee M, Hasbun R
results.key_finding: Streptococcus pneumoniae remains the leading cause of bacterial meningitis in adults. Asplenic patients are at markedly increased risk due to impaired opsonization of encapsulated organisms. Case fatality rate remains 20-30%.

results.title: Duration of antibiotic therapy for pneumococcal meningitis
results.authors: Havens PL, Wendelberger KJ, Denver DR
results.key_finding: Recommended duration for pneumococcal meningitis is 10-14 days of IV antibiotics. Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg every 6 hours for 4 days should be initiated before or with the first dose of antibiotics to reduce mortality and hearing loss.

output.summary: Current guidelines recommend empiric vancomycin plus ceftriaxone (plus ampicillin for Listeria coverage in patients >50 years) for community-acquired bacterial meningitis. Adjunctive dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg q6h for 4 days) should be given before or concurrently with the first antibiotic dose to reduce mortality and sensorineural hearing loss, particularly in pneumococcal meningitis. Duration of therapy for confirmed pneumococcal meningitis is 10-14 days. Asplenic patients are at high risk due to impaired clearance of encapsulated organisms and should receive post-treatment vaccination counseling.

output.proposed: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days
interactions: Metformin + Dexamethasone: Corticosteroids antagonize the hypoglycemic effect of metformin. Monitor blood glucose closely and adjust insulin coverage as needed.
Aspirin + Dexamethasone: Increased risk of GI bleeding with concurrent corticosteroid and antiplatelet therapy. Consider GI prophylaxis.
warnings: Dexamethasone may mask fever, complicating clinical monitoring of treatment response
May worsen hyperglycemia in diabetic patients — frequent blood glucose monitoring recommended
Hold metformin during acute illness with elevated creatinine to reduce risk of lactic acidosis

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 6-7 Hz theta activity replacing the normal alpha rhythm, consistent with diffuse encephalopathy

findings.frequency: 5-7 Hz theta range predominant, intermittent 2-3 Hz delta activity in frontal regions

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to altered mental status

output.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating moderate diffuse encephalopathy characterized by generalized theta-delta slowing. No epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures identified. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Brief 30-minute recording. Patient cooperation limited by altered mental status.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcal meningitis)

differential.likelihood: Initially considered due to gait disturbance, cognitive decline, and communicating hydrocephalus on imaging, but acute deterioration with fever, meningeal signs, and CSF findings inconsistent with NPH
Considered given subacute presentation and asplenic status, but negative India ink, negative cryptococcal antigen, and neutrophilic CSF pleocytosis argue against this
Considered due to subacute course and lymphocytic component of CSF, but negative AFB stain and TB PCR, plus positive pneumococcal antigen, effectively exclude this
Lower likelihood given neutrophilic predominance, very low glucose, high protein, and positive bacterial antigen testing
Low — no known malignancy, leptomeningeal enhancement now explained by infection, but was reasonably considered on imaging differential

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT head to rule out mass lesion or herniation risk before lumbar puncture
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Communicating hydrocephalus without mass lesion, safe to proceed with LP

optimal_actions.action: Administer empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin) and adjunctive IV dexamethasone immediately, ideally before or concurrent with LP
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, and opening pressure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Turbid CSF, elevated opening pressure, neutrophilic pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose, positive pneumococcal antigen

optimal_actions.action: Obtain blood cultures before starting antibiotics
Order comprehensive blood work including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, coagulation studies, and blood glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukocytosis with left shift, elevated CRP and procalcitonin, hyponatremia, hyperglycemia

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with contrast to evaluate for complications such as empyema, cerebritis, or infarction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leptomeningeal enhancement with communicating hydrocephalus, no abscess or empyema

optimal_actions.action: Order CSF meningitis/encephalitis multiplex PCR panel for rapid pathogen identification
Check drug interactions for dexamethasone with current medications, especially metformin and aspirin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dexamethasone-metformin interaction requiring glucose monitoring, aspirin-dexamethasone GI risk

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on bacterial meningitis management in asplenic patients
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Guidelines for empiric therapy, dexamethasone adjunctive therapy, duration of treatment

optimal_actions.action: Request repeat lumbar puncture at 48 hours to assess treatment response
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improving CSF parameters with decreasing WBC, improving glucose

optimal_actions.action: Order audiometry to assess for sensorineural hearing loss as complication of pneumococcal meningitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, worse on left

critical_actions: Administer empiric IV antibiotics (including ceftriaxone and vancomycin) without delay
Administer adjunctive IV dexamethasone before or with first dose of antibiotics
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis (or ensure imaging first if focal deficits present)
Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics if possible without delaying treatment
Recognize asplenic status as high-risk factor for encapsulated organism infection
Add ampicillin for Listeria coverage given patient age over 50
contraindicated_actions: Delaying empiric antibiotics to await imaging or CSF results
Performing lumbar puncture without prior imaging in a patient with altered mental status and possible increased intracranial pressure
Prescribing only oral antibiotics for suspected bacterial meningitis
Omitting dexamethasone in suspected pneumococcal meningitis
Assuming normal pressure hydrocephalus without ruling out infectious meningitis given acute deterioration and fever
key_reasoning_points: The subacute presentation with gait disturbance and cognitive decline initially mimics normal pressure hydrocephalus, which was the initial working diagnosis based on imaging findings of communicating hydrocephalus
The acute deterioration with fever, meningeal signs, and altered sensorium signals a new or previously unrecognized infectious process superimposed on the chronic presentation
The patient's asplenic status (post-splenectomy for ITP) and diabetes create significant immunocompromise, particularly for encapsulated organisms like Streptococcus pneumoniae

Prior partial treatment with oral amoxicillin-clavulanate explains the negative Gram stain but does not exclude bacterial meningitis — the latex agglutination test for pneumococcal antigen remains positive despite antibiotics
CSF profile showing neutrophilic pleocytosis, markedly elevated protein, and critically low glucose with very low CSF-to-serum glucose ratio (0.14) is characteristic of bacterial meningitis
The communicating hydrocephalus may be secondary to meningeal inflammation impairing CSF resorption, rather than representing idiopathic NPH

Cryptococcal and tuberculous meningitis must be excluded in an immunocompromised patient with subacute meningitis and hydrocephalus — negative India ink, cryptococcal antigen, AFB stain, and TB PCR effectively exclude these
Source case (PMC10598285) was cryptococcal meningitis presenting as NPH — this case uses the same initial diagnostic challenge but pivots to bacterial meningitis confirmed by pneumococcal antigen

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty: subacute presentation initially mimicking NPH, with partial antibiotic treatment confounding Gram stain results, asplenic patient at high risk for encapsulated organisms, and hydrocephalus creating diagnostic uncertainty between infectious vs idiopathic etiology. Key diagnostic clues require integrating CSF findings with clinical context rather than relying on a single pathognomonic test.
